errors: The colonoscopy screening logic incorrectly uses a 1‑month look‑back period and checks for the absence of a colonoscopy within that month to trigger a recommendation. Clinically, colonoscopy screening is recommended every 10 years for adults aged 50–75, not every month. This mismatch between the ELM implementation and standard colon cancer screening guidelines renders the values clinically unreasonable.
The HbA1c threshold is set to 9 g% – the unit “g%” is incorrect (HbA1c is expressed as a percentage, e.g., “%”) and the numeric value 9 % is far above the typical diagnostic threshold (≈ 6.5 %–7 %).

The ELM logic does not enforce the 20 pack‑year smoking history requirement that the recommendation and rationale reference. The “Has Smoking History” expression only checks for an active Condition coded as “Tobacco User,” which does not capture pack‑year exposure, leading to a clinically inaccurate screening trigger.

The age range specified (60–75 years) does not align with USPSTF guidelines, which recommend colorectal cancer screening for adults aged 45–75 years. Additionally, the screening interval of 2 years for any colorectal screening is not consistent with modality‑specific intervals (e.g., colonoscopy every 10 years, FIT every 1 year).

The algorithm requires patients to be at least 25 years old to trigger screening, whereas the USPSTF recommendation cited in the rationale applies to adults 18 years and older. This age threshold mismatch makes the clinical logic inconsistent with the cited guideline.
[The age range used in the ELM (55–75 years) does not match the USPSTF guideline, which recommends AAA screening for men aged 65–75 years who have ever smoked.]

The ELM logic does not enforce the upper age limit of 80 years or the 20 pack‑year smoking history requirement that are specified in the USPSTF guideline. The “In Demographic” expression only checks for age ≥ 50, and the “Has Smoking History” expression only verifies an active tobacco user condition, not a 20 pack‑year history.
